State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • •  Administration • •  Executive/Legal • •  Enforcement • •  Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660        322-5901        322-6441        322-6444

March 11, 1981

Edmund A. Duggan
San Rafael City Attorney
1400 Fifth Avenue
San Rafael, CA 94901

Dear Mr. Duggan:

I have received your requests for advice and copies of
your petition for a writ of prohibition filed in the First
Appellate Division. I have reviewed these materials and I
am afraid I must inform you that the Commission does not
intend to intervene in the litigation between yourself and
Mr. Smith. The Act provides, Government Code Section 91003(a),1/
that the court may in its discretion require a plaintiff to
file a complaint with the Commission prior to seeking injunc-
tive relief. The Commission has not interpreted this as a
mandatory requirement upon plaintiffs in injunctive or declara-
tory actions. The only circumstance in which a plaintiff is
required to go first to the Commission (or to the civil
prosecutor) is when a civil action is brought seeking damages
or a penalty for a past violation of the Act. Sections
91004, 91005, 91007. As I read the pleadings, no such claim
has been made by Mr. Smith.

With respect to the specific allegations of Mr. Smith's
complaint, I should point out that the Political Reform Act
only requires disqualification in the case of those individual
decisions in which a public official has a financial interest.
Section 87100. Section 87100 does not provide for removal
of a public official from his official position. Whether a
situation could arise in which a disqualification would be
required so frequently that a local official could not ade-
quately perform his or her duties is not a question for the
Commission.

You have also inquired about certain more specific
factual situations. In view of the adversarial relationship
that you appear to have with Mr. Smith, I do not think that
we can treat you as Mr. Smith's representative for purposes

1/  All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise stated.

Edmund A. Duggan
March 11, 1981
Page Two


of seeking advice relating to the Political Reform Act.
Therefore, under our regulations, I am afraid we cannot give
advice at your request as to whether or not Mr. Smith should
or should not disqualify himself on any particular vote. 2
Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329(b). Advice regarding Mr. Nixon
raises similar problems at this time. Of course, you may
always file a complaint with our Enforcement Division if you
believe a violation of the Act has occurred.

I am sorry that I cannot be of more help to you in this
situation.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Chief Counsel
Legal Division

BAM:kp

CITY OF SAN RAFAEL

SAN RAFAEL, CALIFORNIA

INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMORANDUM


DATE: March 6, 1981

TO: Jack G. Nixon, Chairman, San Rafael Redevelopment Agency

FROM: Edmund A. Duggan, City Attorney

SUBJECT: Conflict of Interest Concerning the
Third Street Project Area


I am in receipt of a copy of a letter to Mr. Beyer from
the Fair Political Practices Commission, Attorney, Barbara
Milman. Ms. Milman states that Mr. Rosenthal has correctly
pointed out the law in the area. However, Ms. Milman goes
on to say, "it's not always easy to determine in the
specific factual situation whether disqualification is
necessary". Then Ms. Milman goes on to say, as I under-
stand it, no advice is expressly being requested on this
set of facts.

Based on that situation, I would respectfully suggest this
matter be put over and the facts be submitted to the F.P.P.C.
for evaluation and decision. The facts should be as
suggested in my February 2, 1981 memo to you.

It is my opinion that the factual situation with respect
to the project area and its impact on McPhail's is fraught
with the potential for a legal conflict of interest.
Prudence would dictate that this matter be truly investi-
gated and determined before action is taken.


enclosure
cc: F.P.P.C., Ms. Barbara Milman

CITY OF SAN RAFAEL
SAN RAFAEL, CALIFORNIA

INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMORANDUM


DATE: February 2, 1981

TO: Jack G. Nixon, Councilmember

FROM: Edmund A. Duggan, City Attorney

SUBJECT: Conflict of Interest Concerning the
Third Street Project Area


You requested my comments on the opinion dated January 13,
1980 (sic), prepared by Mr. Rosenthal (copy attached)
regarding a possible conflict of interest concerning yourself
with respect to voting on a Negative Declaration for the
Third Street improvement project.

My comments are as follows:

McPhail's:

Mr. Rosenthal's analysis lacks an adequate factual foundation
to support his conclusion.

Whether the proposed decision will have a "material financial
effect" on McPhail's can be shown if the decision will
increase or decrease the gross revenue of McPhail's by the
lesser of $100,000 or 1%. If net revenues are calculated a
"material financial effect" on McPhail's can be shown if the
decision will increase or decrease the net revenues by the
lesser of $50,000 or 1/2% (2 Cal. Ad. Code §18702).

Mr. Rosenthal's analysis makes no effort to try to find out
what, if any, change in McPhail's revenue resulted from the
removal of parking on Third Street and the addition of a
parking lot across the street from McPhail's.

In view of the fact that this decision has already been
made, certainly McPhail's has the necessary revenue informa-
tion which they may wish to make available. In the absence
of direct information from McPhail's, the question of making
assumptions concerning McPhail's should be given to the
Planning Department who can estimate, based on the varied
statistical information available to them, whether or not
the McPhail's business had the requisite increase or decrease
in gross or net income.

OTHER POSSIBLE CONFLICTS

With respect to other possible conflicts in the project
area, the opinion gives no advice.

The Fair Political Practices Commission, in the January 15,
1981 letter to the undersigned, states:

"In summary, if Mr. Nixon's pro rata share of gross receipts
paid by a client or customer to a business entity of which
he and his spouse have a 10% or greater interest is between
$250 and $10,000, Mr. Nixon is required to disqualify
himself from any decision which will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on that source of income, but until he determines,
with or without the advice of counsel, that disqualification
is required, he is not obliged to disclose that client or
customer."

Obviously, the key to the requirement to disclose is whether
a governmental decision by you will have a material financial
effect distinguishable from its effect in the public generally
in business entities located in the project area.

Two Fair Political Practices Commission cases are on point:

2 Fair Political Practices Commission 77 (June 2, 1976.)
City of Davis 23 square block "core area"

A Planning Commissioner owns a home across the street from
the boundary of the core area; he can vote.

Another Planning Commissioner owns a vacant lot in the core
area; he cannot vote.

A Councilmember is a limited partner in a retail store
within the core area; he can vote.

4 Fair Political Practices Commission 19 (February 7, 1978.)
City of San Clemente 6 to 7 square block "improvement area"

One Councilmember owns his own law office and another owns a
locksmith business in the improvement district and it is
ruled that neither can vote.

The size of the district and its relation to the rest of the
town appears to be the key to whether or not a governmental
decision for a downtown business area will have a material
financial effect distinguishable from the public generally.

In the San Clemente case at page 3, the Fair Political
Practices Commission states:

"In this case, the relevant category of downtown commercial
property owners is a small one relative to either the class
of all San Clemente commercial property owners or the entire
San Clemente business community. Approximately 50% of the
commercial property in the city is located outside the
proposed assessment district. As in the case of the Davis
commercial lessors, we do not believe that the class of
downtown commercial property owners can be considered a
significant segment of the public. Because the improvement
project is limited to the downtown area, commercial property
in the improvement district will reap direct benefits and
incur district costs that will not be shared by other
commercial property in the city. In fact, increased downtown
business and the concomitant increase in downtown property
values may be gained at the expense of commercial property
in other parts of the city. Therefore, the effect of the
proposed decisions will be distinguishable from their effect
on the public generally. Consequently, the two councilmembers
must disqualify themselves from decisions concerning creation
of the improvement district."

It appears to me that the small size of the San Rafael
project area and the nature of the improvements being made
make the business entities in the project area distinguishable
from the public generally.

Pursuant to the foregoing, I conclude that if you have income
of $250 or more from a business entity within the project area
and a governmental decision you make will have a significant
financial effect on such entity as detailed above you must not
vote and you must also disclose the disqualifying financial
interest. The Fair Political Practices Commission, in their
letter of January 15, 1981, states:

"... if Mr. Nixon decides that he has a conflict of interest
in a particular decision within the meaning of the Political
Reform Act, his determination not to participate in that
decision must be accompanied by disclosure of the financial
interest, made part of the Redevelopment Agency's official
record. 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18700(b) (5). If he has a conflict
because the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on
a source of income of less than $10,000 to his business
entity, then this disclosure at the time of disqualification will result in the disclosure of the name of a disqualified
client or customer who was not required to be reported on
his statement of economic interests."

If you have any questions, please call me.

Respectfully submitted,

Edmund A. Duggan
City Attorney

cc: Leo Rosenthal, Esq.
Robert P. Beyer, City Manager
[illegible] M. Iossinni, City Clerk

January 22, 1981

Ms. Barbara Milman
Conflicts of Interest - Legal Division
State of California Fair Political Practices Division
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Ms. Milman:

This letter is in follow-up to my conversation with you on
January 22, 1981, asking if you would comment on the enclosed
letter by Mr. Lee C. Rosenthall concerning the matter of potential conflict of interest of Mr. Jack Nixon, Chairman of the
San Rafael Redevelopment Agency.

The purpose of making this inquiry is to ascertain, essentially,
if you agree with the opinion given to Mr. Nixon by Mr. Rosenthall.
I would appreciate any comments you might have on this opinion.
If you could respond prior to January 30th, it would be most
appreciated.

Thank you for your assistance in this matter.

Very truly yours,

ROBERT P. BEYLER
City Manager

FEB 2 1981

CITY ATTORNEY
CITY OF SAN RAFAEL

LAW OFFICES OF

GOLDFARB & OWENS

STEVEN H. GOLDFARB

1110 BROADWAY, SUITE 825

TELEPHONE

THOMAS R. OWENS

OAKLAND, CALIFORNIA 94612

(415) 839-6336

BARRY A. LIPMAN

H DAVID KROOT

JOYCE S. A. TISCHLER

LEE C. ROSENTHAL

R. ZACHARY WASSERMAN

January 13, 1980

RECEIVED

Robert Beyer
Executive Director
San Rafael Redevelopment Agency
City Hall
1400 Fifth Avenue
San Rafael, CA 94901

JAN 13 1981

CITY ATTORNEY
CITY OF SAN RAFAEL

Dear Mr. Beyer:

At the request of the Agency, I am writing with regard to
the alleged conflict of interest of Agency member Jack Nixon
in the matter of the negative declaration on the 3rd Street
Improvement project.

The facts as I understand them are as follows: Mr. Nixon
is an insurance salesman. In his insurance business, Mr. Nixon
has received income of more than $250 in the past year from
commissions on the sale of insurance policies to McPhails.
McPhails has a retail business that is located at the corner of
3rd and C Streets. The entrance of McPhails is on C Street, and
it has adequate off-street parking at the rear of its building.

The decision about which the conflict of interest question
arises involves issuance of a negative declaration under the
California Environmental Quality Act with respect to the 3rd Street
Improvement project. This project involves elimination of about
50 parking spaces on 3rd Street and making certain improvements in
the traffic flow on that street. Of the 50 spaces eliminated,
approximately 15 are within a block of McPhails. The project was
proposed and approved by the Agency in 1978 allegedly without com-
pliance with the California Environmental Quality Act ("CEQA").
Owners of businesses on 3rd Street then brought suit against the
Agency seeking to overturn the approval on grounds that CEQA was
not complied in approving the project. At the request of the owners,
the Superior Court issued a preliminary injunction preventing imple-
mentation of the project. The Agency then certified a negative
declaration for the project, and the court thereafter dissolved the
preliminary injunction. After the injunction was dissolved, the Agency
went forward with and completed the project in 1979.

Trial of the owners' suit was held in 1980, and the trial court

RECEIVED
[illegible]

Robert Beyer
January 12, 1980
Page 2

judge ordered the Agency to make a new determination as to whether
or not a negative declaration for the project should be issued.
In its order the court specifically ordered the Agency to consider
the elimination of parking on 3rd Street in light of other projects
that have been undertaken to provide off-street parking in the
vicinity of the 3rd Street area. It is this new negative declaration
determination that raises the conflict of interest question for
Mr. Nixon.

Under the Political Reform Act of 1974, an official cannot
participate in a decision if it is "reasonably forseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally," on a source of income
from whom the official has received $250 in income in the previous
year. Government Code Section 87103.

Under the peculiar circumstances of this case, it is not reasonably
forseeable that the decision with regard to the negative declaration
will have a material financial effect upon Mr. Nixon's source of
income, McPhails. To both the decision before the Agency is not the
substantive decision as to whether or not to do the project, but
rather a procedural step, i.e. whether or not to prepare an environ-
mental impact report. That decision standing alone will have no
financial effect on McPhails. In normal circumstances, the decision
whether or not to prepare an environmental impact report is made as
a preliminary step in the process of determining whether to undertake
a project. In those circumstances the decision concerning preparation
of the impact report conceivably could have a material financial
effect if it is reasonably forseeable that the report will impact the
substantive decision regarding the project itself.

In this instance, however, the decision regarding the project
has already been made, and the project itself has been carried out.
Therefore, it is highly unlikely that an environmental impact report
will affect the substantive decision to carry out the project. See
Residents Ad Hoc Stadium Comm. v. Board of Trustees, 89 Ca.App.3d 274
(1979).

Therefore, it cannot be said that it is reasonably forseeable
that the decision regarding the negative declaration will affect the
decision with regard to the project in such a manner so as to have
a material financial effect on McPhails.

Even if we were dealing here with the substantive decision to
proceed with the project, it would be difficult to conclude that the
decision would have a material financial effect upon McPhails. The
project eliminated parking on 3rd Street and somewhat altered the
traffic flow on that street. It is not reasonably forseeable that

Robert Beyer
January 13, 1980
Page 3

removal of spaces will materially affect McPhails. The business is
not oriented to 3rd Street but rather to C Street, and it has its
own off-street parking. Thus, it is not foreseeable that the elimin-
ation of the few spaces on 3rd Street in the immediate vicinity of
McPhails would materially affect the volume of customers going to
McPhails. Therefore, the income of the business would not be
materially affected.

It should be noted that regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission define "material financial effect" as an effect of $100,000
or 1% per year on gross income or $50,000 or 1/2% per year on net
income. 2 Cal.Adm. Code Section 18703. It is highly unlikely that
the elimination of parking on 3rd Street will have the magnitude of
effect on McPhails to be considered material given the fact that
McPhails has sales of five to ten million dollars or more a year.

The other aspect of the project - improvement of traffic flow
in the downtown area of San Rafael - may affect McPhails in that
circulation in the area will be improved. However, all businesses
in the downtown area will be affected in substantially the same manner
by improved circulation. The segment of the population consisting
of all downtown businesses is large enough to be considered the
"public generally" for purposes of determining whether a material
financial effect on a business is distinguishable from the effect
on the public generally. In opinion Requested by William H. Owen,
City Attorney, City of Davis, 2 FPPC Ops.77 (1976). Thus, even if
the traffic aspect of the decision affects McPhails materially, Mr.
Nixon need not disqualify himself since the decision affects all
downtown businesses in the same manner.

With respect to Mr. Duggan's letter of January 5, 1980, to
Mr. Nixon, a comment is appropriate. Contrary to Duggan's
letter, the California Supreme Court, in Hayes v. Wood, 25 Cal.3d
772 (1979), struck down Government Code Section 87207 (b)(2) as
violative of equal protection and held that the threshold for dis-
closure of income by brokers and attorneys should be that provided
in Government Code Section 87207 (b)(3) for other professions, i.e.,
$10,000. Thus, contrary to the statements in Mr. Duggan's letter,
Mr. Nixon's threshold for disclosure of a source of income from
his insurance business on a statement of economic interest is
$10,000 in a year, not $1,000 in a year.

In his letter, Mr. Duggan also notes the construction of parking
lots across from McPhails as a possible source of a conflict of
interest. As I understand it, the construction of those lots was
not part of the project for which the original negative declaration
was issued. The effect of those lots obviously relates to the
environmental impact of removal of parking in that the impact of
removal is mitigated by availability of alternative parking. This

Robert Beyer
January 13, 1980
Page 4

issue is discussed in both the 1978 and 1980 initial studies for the
negative declaration. However, I do not see the 1980 negative
declaration decision as being one prepared for the decision to build
that off-street parking. Furthermore, even if the "project" for
which the 1980 negative declaration is prepared is viewed as both
the removal of parking from 3rd Street and the off-street parking,
it is not forseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect on McPhails. Because the Agency has already purchased the land
for and constructed the lots, it is highly unlikely that a decision
regarding the negative declaration will have any impact on a decision
that has already been made and implemented at a large cost to the
Agency.

Sincerely,

[signature]
LEE C. ROSENTHAL

LCR:mm
cc: Ed Ward
William T. Bullard, Jr.✓

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance •• Administration •• Executive Legal •• Enforcement •• Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5660        322-5901        322-6441        322-6444

January 15, 1981


Edmund A. Duggan
San Rafael City Attorney
1400 Fifth Avenue
San Rafael, CA 94902

A-81-01-087

Dear Mr. Duggan:

     This is in response to your letter of January 9, 1981, in
which you have requested us to confirm the advice you provided
to Jack Nixon, Chairman of the San Rafael Redevelopment Agency,
by memorandum dated January 5, 1980 [sic]. In that memorandum
you advised Mr. Nixon that he is required to disclose clients
who have paid him as little as $250 in the last twelve months.
I am afraid I cannot agree with that conclusion.

     It is true that the Political Reform Act requires public
officials to refrain from making, participating in making, or
using their official position to influence any decision which
will have a foreseeable material financial effect distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on sources of income
of $250 or more within twelve months prior to the time the
decision is made. Government Code Section 87103. However, with
respect to sources of income to a business entity, assuming a
filer and his spouse own more than 10% of the entity, disclosure
(as distinguished from disqualification) is required only of
those clients and customers of the business entity who pay so
much in gross receipts to the business entity that the filer's
pro rata share is $10,000 or more. This is the disclosure
required of persons specified in Government Code Section 87200
by Government Code Section 87207(b), and Government Code Section
87302(b) provides that the same information shall be disclosed
by designated employees filing under Conflict of Interest Codes,
subject of course to the disclosure categories applicable to
them in the Code. Note that the decision in Hays v. Wood did not
uphold the $1,000 threshold for attorneys and brokers. Con-
sequently, they like all other filers must now disclose at the
$10,000 level.

Mr. Edmund A. Duggan
January 15, 1981
Page Two

     Finally, if Mr. Nixon decides that he has a conflict of
interest in a particular decision within the meaning of the
Political Reform Act, his determination not to participate in
that decision must be accompanied by disclosure of the financial
interest, made part of the Redevelopment Agency's official
record. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(b)(5). If he has a
conflict because the decision will have a material financial
effect distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on a source of income of less than $10,000 to his business entity,
then this disclosure at the time of disqualification will result
in the disclosure of the name of a client or customer who was
not required to be reported on his statement of economic interests.

     In summary, if Mr. Nixon's pro rata share of gross receipts
paid by a client or customer to a business entity of which he
and his spouse have a 10% or greater interest in between $250
and $10,000, Mr. Nixon is required to disqualify himself from
any decision which will have a material financial effect, dis-
tinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on that
source of income, but until he determines, with or without the
advice of counsel, that disqualification is required, he is not
obliged to disclose that client or customer.

     If you have any further questions concerning this matter,
please do not hesitate to contact us again.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [illegible]
                                        Sarah T. Cameron
                                        Deputy Chief for
                                          Conflicts of Interest
                                        Legal Division

MAYOR
LAWRENCE E MULRYAN
COUNCIL MEMBERS
OSCAR EWING SKINNER
FRED JENSEN
JOHN A. MISKIMEN
JACK G. NIXON

January 9, 1981

Sara Cameron, Esq.
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804

Re:  Request for Confirmation of Correctness of Advice Given
     to San Rafael Redevelopment Agency Chairman Jack Nixon

Dear Attorney Cameron,

Please find enclosed a memorandum prepared by me to Jack Nixon,
Chairman of the San Rafael Redevelopment Agency. It is my
position that before Mr. Nixon can vote on the matter at hand
he must reveal, for factual and legal evaluation, income ex-
ceeding $250 from persons or businesses within the project
area.  The City Council has requested an opinion from special
Redevelopment Agency counsel on this matter.  I have enclosed
their request as well for your inspection.

The next meeting of the Agency is on January 19, 1981.  I
would appreciate a letter from you confirming the correctness
of the memorandum I provided to Mr. Nixon or, in the alterna-
tive, any comments you might have with respect to any error
or omission in my memorandum.

If you have any questions, please call me.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Edmund A. Duggan
                                        City Attorney

F A/15

Enclosures

CITY OF SAN RAFAEL

SAN RAFAEL, CALIFORNIA

INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMORANDUM


HAND DELIVERED                                           DATE: January 5, 1980

TO:    Jack Nixon, Chairman, San Rafael Redevelopment Agency

FROM:  Edmund A. Duggan, City Attorney

SUBJECT: Possible Conflict of Interest in
         Third Street Project Area


     I am in receipt of a letter from attorneys Bernstein and
Peterson alleging that you have an unspecified conflict of
interest with respect to the Third Street project area.

     I understand you were contacted by Mr. Bullard and were not
able to offer any comment as to what conflict Messers.
Bernstein and Peterson are referring to.

     I have reviewed your latest Fair Political Practices Commission Form 721 for January 1, 1979 to December 31, 1979, in
which you declare having income in excess of $1,000 from
McPhail's which has a major outlet at Third and "C" Streets
which is within the Third Street project area.

     Whether this or any other interest you might have in the
project area disqualifies you from voting is a mixed question
of fact and law which should be analyzed by City Planners
and the office of the City Attorney before a vote is taken.

     Should you vote without the necessary analysis, the action
taken will be subject to court challenge and may result in
the action of the Redevelopment Agency being set aside or
being declared void pursuant to §91003(b) of the Government
Code.

     You may, of course, avoid the problem by abstaining from any
further participation or vote in the matter.

ANALYSIS

     You are required to disclose income annually pursuant to
§87207(b) of the Government Code which provides in pertinent
part:

     "... (2) In the case of a business entity
     which provides legal or brokerage services,
     the name of every person who paid fees to
     the business entity if the filer's pro rata
     share of fees from such person was equal to
     or greater than one thousand dollars ($1,000)."

Chairman Jack Nixon
January 5, 1980
Page Two


This section was upheld in Hays v. Wood 25 C 3d 772, 160
Cal. Rptr. 102, and a procedure was established to withhold
the identity of a source of income. In any case, an income
statement for 1980 is not yet due (see Government Code
§87203) and it is unknown if you had the same income from
McPhail's in 1980 as you did in 1979. You are required to
disqualify yourself from voting where a governmental
decision is to be made in which you have a financial interest
(Government Code §87100).

A financial interest is present "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally on
... any source of income ... aggregating $250.00 or more in
value ... to the public official ...." (Government Code §87103).

This means that if you have clients in the Third Street
project area which you have not previously disclosed because
the income to you was less than $1,000, you must now disclose
any such clients who paid you as little as $250 in the
last 12 months. Such income may prove, upon analysis, to
disqualify you from voting. The more than $1,000 income
from McPhail's, if again received in 1980, appears to
disqualify you from voting on this matter for three reasons:

1. It exceeds $250.

2. McPhail's is directly impacted by the plan due to its
location at Third and "C" Streets.

3. Substantial enhancement to the McPhail property by the
addition by the Redevelopment Agency of a parking lot
lot next to the McPhail store.

The Planning Department analysis of the financial effect will
allow legal guidelines to be applied which will provide a
basis to judge whether or not a material financial effect on
your income is foreseeable.

Respectfully submitted,

Edmund A. Duggan
City Attorney

cc: Redevelopment Agency Members
Redevelopment Agency Executive Director
Agency Secretary
Messrs. Bernstein and Peterson (Hand Delivered)

M B/17

CITY
OF
SAN RAFAEL

1400 FIFTH AVE, SAN RAFAEL, CALIF 94902/PHONE: (415)456-1112

MAYOR
LAWRENCE E MULRYAN
COUNCIL MEMBERS
DOROTHY L BREIMER
FRED JENSEN
GARY F SWEETEN
JACK G NIXON

Lile

January 6, 1984

Lee C. Rosenthal, Esq.
Goldfarb & Owens Law Offices
1333 Broadway, Suite 825
Oakland, California 94612

Re: Legal Opinion Regarding Agency Chairman Jack Nixon -
Third Street Improvement Project - Negative Declaration

Dear Lee:

Following the public hearing regarding the captioned matter, the Redevelopment
Agency informally decided to postpone taking formal action pending the outcome
of a legal opinion to be drafted which addresses the ability of Redevelopment
Agency Chairman Jack Nixon to participate in the referenced matter taking
into consideration the fact that McPhail's (which has a retail outlet bor-
dering on Third Street) is a source of income for Mr. Nixon.

Although, strictly speaking, Mr. Nixon's true sources of income are the
principal agents, i.e., the large insurance companies, he has nonetheless
listed those customers or clients from whom he receives more than a $1,000
annually in income. McPhail's is one such source of income. The retail store
in San Rafael on Third and C Streets is but one part of the entity which com-
prises McPhail's, a Marin County building supplier. (See enclosed description
from 1 FPPC Opinions 199, December 4, 1975.)

Because of a prior opinion rendered by your office in this matter (see enclosed
letter) we feel it appropriate for you again to render an opinion concerning
this complex and difficult area of law.

If you require further information as a factual basis for your opinion, do not
hesitate to contact either myself or Mr. Nixon at 472-0232.

Thank you for your assistance.

Very truly yours,

William T. Bullard, Jr
Assistant Agency Attorney

NO. 75-089

1 FPPC OPINIONS 199

which discussions are expected to lead eventually to a vote
on whether the moratorium should be lifted and, if so, when.

Director Jack MacPhafl is the executive vice-president,
a salaried employee and a minority stockholder of MacPhafl's,
Inc., a closely held family corporation. He and his family
control 50 percent of the outstanding stock in the corporation.

MacPhafl's does business almost entirely within Sonoma
and Marin Counties, and does over half of its business within
the MMWD. It has gross sales of between $5,000,000 and
$10,000,000 annually, and earns an annual after-tax profit of
less than two percent on sales. The business of McPhafl's
consists of: (A) the sale of ready-mix concrete and building
materials, such as bulk aggregates, rebar steel, cast patio
materials, decorator rock, and sacked materials (sand, cement,
lime, concrete mix, vermiculite, etc.), both to contractors
and at retail; (b) the sale of major appliances, such as
washers, dryers, dish-washers, stoves, refrigerators, television
sets, etc., both to builder-jobbers and at retail; (c) the
sale of fuels, propane, heating oil and road oil, principally
at retail; and (d) the sale of heating, air conditioning and
sheet metal products, both at wholesale and at retail.

McPhafl's has numerous competitors in all of those areas,
except ready-mix concrete, where it has only three major competitors. Its estimated relative market share within the MMWD
of the foregoing products is: ready-mix concrete -- less than
33-1/3 percent; building materials -- less than 25 percent;
major appliances -- less than 20 percent; fuels -- over 50
percent of bottled gas, but less than two percent of the total
market since most homes are served natural gas by Pacific Gas
and Electric; heating, air conditioning and sheet metal products --
less than five percent.

Whenever an applicant for water for any project comes
before the Board of Directors of the MMWD, Mr. MacPhafl may
be facing one of the following situations:

(a) McPhafl's has no known connection with the
project, but may later bid on or supply for the
project ready-mix, building materials, appliances and/or fuel;

(b) McPhafl's is preparing or has made a bid to
supply one or more of its products, but no
award has been made;

(c) A contractor, who is a regular customer of
McPhafl's and who normally buys principally


3/78

'ney members

LAW OFFICES OF

GOLDFARB & OWENS
THOMAS M OWENS
STEVEN H GOLDFARB
MARK H GAINER

A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
1333 BROADWAY, SUITE 825
OAKLAND, CALIFORNIA 94612

TELEPHONE
(415) 838-8336

March 5, 1976

Mr. William Bielseer
City Manager
City of San Rafael
1400 Fifth
San Rafael, CA

Dear Bill:

I am writing this in response to your telephone request this afternoon concerning the question of conflict of interest as it related to Agency conceptual action on the ROMA study. As I understand from you, the specific concern raised here is a question as to whether or not a council member whose business is that of an insurance broker would be precluded from voting in matters relating to land use study presently being prepared by ROMA inasmuch as the study deals with land utilization in Downtown San Rafael which private property may be insured by a member of the Council.

As I am sure you are aware, a determination as to what is a conflict under Proposition Nine is an extremely difficult one to make and to date the Commission itself has failed to promulgate determinative regulations in regard to the conflict issue. As such, each decision must be made on a case by case basis. If one were to take Proposition Nine to its ultimate absurdity, it is clear that the fact that one insures property within the City could create a conflict under Proposition Nine. The result of this would be that no person could serve on the council who generally did business within the City, for clearly every decision will have some effect on one's business. The determining factor here is whether the decision would have a material financial effect. Without knowing the specifics of the properties insured by any member of the council and given the fact that the ROMA study is just that - a study - it would be my opinion that upon disclosure of the business interests involved, the Council person could participate in decisions relating to the conceptual ROMA plan and the land use provisions contained within it.

[illegible]

. [illegible]

Mr. William Bielser
Page Two
3/5/76


It would not be permissible to vote on a matter which specifically related
to a client's property other than in a general nature as it affects the
land use patterns within the Downtown as a whole. For example, if at some
time in order to implement the policies of the Agency as it affected the
Central San Rafael Redevelopment Project Area, decisions had to be made as
to whether or not to acquire specific property, if the Councilperson insured
the building which was under consideration for acquisition, it might well be
a conflict depending on the specific facts of that acquisition to participate
in the decision to acquire. As such it would be my opinion that the Council
person must refrain from participating in such a decision. However, I
believe it is permissible for this Councilperson, based on the facts I have
available to me at this time to participate in the decision-making as it
relates to the land use proposals in the ROMA study provided a complete
disclosure is made at the public meeting at which such participation and
decision-making is to take place as to the types of business relationships
that Councilperson has within the project area relative to his insurance
business.

I realize that I have not provided you with a clear-cut opinion on
conflict of interest at this time. This is partially due to the limited
facts I presently have available to me and even more importantly because
of the lack of guidance presently available from the Fair Political Practices
Commission in regard to conflict of interest. As this matter will become
increasingly important, it would be my suggestion that we get together to
develop specific guidelines for Agency use as it relates to conflict of interest.

Yours very truly,

STEVEN H. GOLDFARB

sections.question: Whether the Fair Political Practices Commission intends to intervene in litigation between the requester and Mr. Smith, and specific questions regarding disqualification requirements and conflict of interest determinations under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion: The Commission does not intend to intervene in the litigation. Section 91003(a) does not mandate that a plaintiff file a complaint with the Commission prior to seeking injunctive relief; such administrative exhaustion is required only when seeking damages or penalties under sections 91004, 91005, 91007. The Political Reform Act requires disqualification only in decisions where a public official has a financial interest (Section 87100), but does not provide for removal from office. The FPPC cannot provide advice on specific factual situations where adversarial relationships exist with the opposing party.
sections.analysis:
You are required to disclose income annually pursuant to 
§87207(b) of the Government Code which provides in pertinent 
part: 
" 
(2) In the case of a business entity 
which provides legal or brokerage services, 
the name of every person who paid fees to 
the business entity if the filer's pro rata 
share of fees from such person was equal to 
or greater than one thousand dollars ($1,000)." 


Chairman Jack Ni 
January 5, 1980 
Page Two 
This section was upheld in Hays v. Wood 25 C 3d 772, 160 
Cal. Rptr. 102, and a procedure was established to withhold 
the identity of a source of income. 
In any case, an income 
statement for 1980 is not yet due (see Government Code 
§87203) and it is unknown if you had the same income from 
MCPhail's in 1980 as you did in 1979. 
You are required to 
dis9ualify yourself from voting where a governmental 
dec1sion is to be made in which you have a financial interest 
(Government Code §87100). 
A financial interest is present "if it is reasonably foresee-
able that the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effect-on the public generally on 
••• any source of income ••• aggregating $250.00 or more in 
value ••• to the public official ••• " (Government Code §87103). 
This mea~s that if you have clients in the Third Street 
project area which you have not previously disclosed because 
the income to you was less than $1,000, you must now disclose 
any such clients who have paid you as little as $250 in the 
last 12 months. 
Such income may prove, upon analysis, to 
disquulify you from voting. 
The more than $1,000 income 
from McPhail's, if again received in 1980, appears to 
disqualify you from voting on this matter for three reasons: 
1. It exceeds $250. 
2. 
MCPhail's is directly impacted by the plan due to its 
location at Third and nCR Streets. 
3. 
Substantial enhancement to the McPhail property by the 
addition by the Redevelopment Agency of a parking lot 
lot next to the McPhail store. 
The Planning Department analysis of the financial effect will 
allow legal guidelines to be applied which will provide a 
basis to judge whether or not a material financial effect on 
your income is foreseeable.

Re: 
Legal Opinion Regarding Agency Chairman Jack Nixon -
Third Street Improvement Project -
Negative Declaration 
Dear Lee: 
Following the public hearing regarding the captioned matter, the Redevelopment 
Agency informally decided to postpone taking formal action pending the outcome 
of a legal opinion to be drafted which addresses the ability of Redevelopment 
Agency Chairman Jack Nixon to participate in the referenced matter, taking 
into consideration the fact that McPhail's (which has a retail outlet bor-
dering on Third Street) is a source of income for Mr. Nixon. 
Although, strictly speaking, Mr. Nixon's true sources of income are the 
principal agents, i.e., the large insurance companies, he has nonetheless 
listed those customers or clients from whom he receives more than a $1,000 
annually in income. 
McPhail's is one such source of income. 
The retail store 
in San Rafael on Third and C Streets is but one part of the entity which com-
prises McPhail's, a Marin County building supplier. 
(See enclosed description 
from 1 FPPC Opinions 199, December 4, 1975.) 
Because of a prior opinion rendered by your office in this matter (see enclosec 
letter) we feel it appropriate for you again to render an opinion concerning 
this complex and difficult area of law. 
If you require further information as a factual basis for your opinion, do not 
hesitate to contact either myself or Mr. Nixon at 472-0232. 
Thank you for your assistance.

sections.question_synthetic: Does the FPPC intend to intervene in pending litigation, and what are the disqualification requirements for public officials with financial interests in matters before them?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC will not intervene in the litigation and clarifies that administrative exhaustion is not required for injunctive relief. Disqualification is required for decisions with financial impact, but removal is not available under the Act, and the FPPC declines to advise on specific factual scenarios involving the adverse party.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document quality is poor with significant OCR errors and image quality degradation affecting readability. Multiple embedded documents (inter-departmental memos, correspondence) are included. Core advice letter sections are identifiable but text is partially corrupted. The main FPPC response letter is dated March 11, 1981, but surrounding documents create context about a San Rafael conflict of interest matter involving the Third Street Project Area.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the Fair Political Practices Commission intends to intervene in litigation between the requester and Mr. Smith, and specific questions regarding disqualification requirements and conflict of interest determinations under the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: The Commission does not intend to intervene in the litigation. Section 91003(a) does not mandate that a plaintiff file a complaint with the Commission prior to seeking injunctive relief; such administrative exhaustion is required only when seeking damages or penalties under sections 91004, 91005, 91007. The Political Reform Act requires disqualification only in decisions where a public official has a financial interest (Section 87100), but does not provide for removal from office. The FPPC cannot provide advice on specific factual situations where adversarial relationships exist with the opposing party.
embedding.first_500_words: /R8 L State of California Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest (916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444 March 11, 1981 Edmund A. Duggan San Rafael City Attorney 1400 Fifth Avenue San Rafael, CA 94901 Dear Mr. Duggan: I have received your requests for advice and copies of your petition for a writ of prohibition filed in the First Appellate Division. I have reviewed these materials and I am afraid I must inform you that the Commission does not intend to intervene in the litigation between yourself and Mr. Smith. The Act provides, Government Code Section 91003(a),1/ that the court may in its discretion require a plaintiff to file a complaint with the Commission prior to seeking injunc- tive relief. The Commission has not interpreted this as a mandatory requirement upon plaintiffs in injunctive or declara- tory actions. The only circumstance in which a plaintiff is required to go first to the Commission (or to the civil prosecutor) is when a civil action is brought seeking damages or a penalty for a past violation of the Act. Sections 91004, 91005, 91007. As I read the pleadings, no such claim has been made by Mr. Smith. With respect to the specific allegations of Mr. Smith's complaint, I should point out that the Political Reform Act only requires disqualification in the case of those individual decisions in which a public official has a financial interest. Section 87100. Section 87100 does not provide for removal of a public official from his official position. Whether a situation could arise in which a disqualification would be required so frequently that a local official could not ade- quately perform his or her duties is not a question for the Commission. You have also inquired about certain more specific factual situations. In view of the adversarial relationship that you appear to have with Mr. Smith, I do not think that we can treat you as Mr. Smith's representative for purposes 1/ All statutory references made are to the Government Code unless otherwise stated. Edmund A. Duggan March 11, 1981 Page Two of seeking advice relating to the Political Reform Act. Therefore, under our regulations, I am afraid we cannot give advice at your request as to whether or not Mr. Smith should or should not disqualify himself on any particular vote. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329(b). Advice regarding Mr. Nixon raises similar problems at this time. Of course, you may always file a complaint with our Enforcement Division if you believe a violation of the Act has occurred. I am sorry that I cannot be of more help to you in this situation. Very truly yours, Barbara A. Milman Chief Counsel Legal Division BAM:kp CITY OF SAN RAFAEL SAN RAFAEL, CALIFORNIA INTER-DEPARTMENTAL MEMORANDUM DATE: March 6, 1981 TO: Jack G. Nixon, Chairman, San Rafael Redevelopment Agency FROM:
embedding.summary: This is a multi-part FPPC advice letter from March 1981 addressing a San Rafael city attorney's requests regarding intervention in litigation, disqualification requirements under the Political Reform Act, and conflict of interest analysis for a redevelopment project. The FPPC declines intervention and advises that administrative exhaustion applies only to damage claims, while clarifying disqualification requirements and declining to advise on specific factual disputes.
